questions.question: Prove formally that any recursive tree traversal visits exactly n nodes in O(n) time for a tree with n nodes.
keyPoints: Base case: Empty tree (0 nodes) takes O(1) — no recursive calls
Inductive step: For node with left subtree of size k and right of size n-k-1, traversal does O(1) work at node plus T(k) + T(n-k-1) for subtrees
Summing work across all nodes: each node contributes exactly one O(1) visit
Total T(n) = O(n) by summing n × O(1) or by induction solving recurrence to n-1 operations
Therefore time complexity is Θ(n) for any depth-first traversal

questions.question: Derive the space complexity of recursive tree traversal and explain why Morris traversal achieves O(1) space.
keyPoints: Recursive traversal: Call stack depth equals tree height h (maximum path length root→leaf)
At any point, at most h+1 stack frames exist (including current node)
Space = O(h) = O(log n) for balanced, O(n) for skewed trees
Morris traversal achieves O(1): temporarily creates threads from nodes to inorder predecessors
Thread creation: if left subtree null, process node; otherwise find rightmost of left (predecessor)
If predecessor's right is null, thread to current node; process left subtree; restore
After processing, all temporary threads removed; original tree restored
No explicit stack or recursion needed; only O(1) extra pointers

questions.question: Given preorder {A,B,D,E,H,I,C,F,J,K,G} and inorder {D,H,E,I,B,J,F,K,C,A,G}, construct the binary tree and verify the postorder sequence.
keyPoints: Preorder first element A = root of entire tree
Inorder split at A: left = {D,H,E,I,B}, right = {J,F,K,C,G}
Continue recursively: B is root of left subtree (preorder[1])
Left of B in inorder: {D,H,E,I} → E is root of B's right, D left, I right of E
Right subtree: C is root (preorder after left), with left={J,F,K} right={G}
Final tree structure confirms postorder: D,H,I,E,B,J,K,F,C,G,A
Verify: Postorder last element = A (root), consistent with tree construction

questions.question: Explain how level-order traversal differs from depth-first traversals in terms of algorithm design, data structures used, and applications.
keyPoints: DFS (pre/in/post): Uses stack (implicit via recursion or explicit); explores depth before breadth
BFS (level-order): Uses queue; explores level by level, breadth before depth
Space: DFS uses O(h), BFS uses O(w) where w ≤ n/2 maximum width
Applications of BFS: Shortest path in unweighted graphs, level-wise processing, finding minimum depth
Applications of DFS: Topological sorting, cycle detection, expression tree evaluation, serialization
BFS requires more space in wide trees; DFS risks stack overflow in deep trees
Time complexity both O(n) — visit each node exactly once

questions.question: Prove that in any binary tree with n nodes, there are exactly n+1 null pointers.
keyPoints: Each binary tree node has exactly 2 pointer fields: left and right
Total pointer slots in tree = 2n
Every node except root has exactly one incoming pointer from its parent
Therefore non-null (actual) pointers = n-1 (all nodes except root have a parent)
By pointer counting: 2n total slots = (n-1) non-null + null pointers
Solving: null pointers = 2n - (n-1) = n + 1
This theorem is fundamental to threaded binary tree design, explaining why n+1 threads can replace all null links

questions.question: Design and analyze an algorithm to find the Lowest Common Ancestor (LCA) of two nodes in a binary tree. Provide both recursive and iterative solutions with complete complexity analysis.
keyPoints: Definition: LCA of nodes p and q is the deepest node that is ancestor of both
Recursive approach: From root, if root is null return null; if p or q equals root, return root
Otherwise recurse on left subtree and right subtree
If both results non-null, current root is LCA (p and q in different subtrees)
If only one non-null, return that result (both nodes in same subtree)
Time: O(n) worst case visiting each node once; Space: O(h) recursion stack
Iterative using parent pointers: Create set of ancestors of p by traversing up storing nodes
Then traverse from q upward checking if ancestor already in set; first match is LCA
Time O(n), Space O(h) for ancestor set
Optimized for BST: Use BST property comparing values to navigate; O(h) time, O(1) space

questions.question: Given a binary tree, explain how you would modify traversals to compute: (a) width of tree at each level, (b) vertical order sum, and (c) diagonal sum. Provide algorithmic details.
keyPoints: (a) Level width: Use level-order with queue tracking level boundaries. Insert marker (null) between levels or store (node, level) pairs. Count nodes per level; track maximum. Time O(n), Space O(w).

(b) Vertical order: For each node, compute horizontal distance from root: root=0, left child=d-1, right child=d+1. Use hashmap: key=distance, value=list of nodes. Inorder traversal populates correct left-to-right order. Also compute sum per column. Time O(n), Space O(n).

(c) Diagonal sum: Nodes on same diagonal share property: row - col is constant (or node index relationship). Use hashmap with key = row-index. Traverse tree (any order), add node value to respective diagonal. For tree where root at (0,0): diagonal d = row - col. Time O(n), Space O(d) where d = number of diagonals.

questions.question: Prove that reconstructing a binary tree from preorder and inorder sequences is always possible and unique. Extend this to analyze scenarios where only one traversal is available.
keyPoints: Proof of uniqueness with preorder+inorder: Preorder[0] is always root. Inorder locates this root, dividing into left/right subtrees. Recursively apply: left subtree's preorder follows root, ending at inorder left portion. By induction, unique tree reconstruction.
Complexity: Finding root in inorder is O(n) with naive search, O(n) total with preprocessing (hashmap). Total: O(n²) naive, O(n) with hashmap. Space O(n).

With only preorder: Cannot determine left/right boundary uniquely. Multiple trees share same preorder (e.g., tree with only right children vs only left children produce different inorder but same preorder).
With only inorder: Insufficient — any tree structure produces same inorder if BST property maintained. Different heights possible.
Preorder + postorder: Can reconstruct if node values unique, but requires additional logic to identify root of subtrees. Otherwise ambiguous for some structures.
